cpt.description: Emergency department visit, high medical decision complexity

carc.description: Non-covered service — payer determined service not medically necessary.
carc.appealNotes: Frequently overturned when admitting diagnosis clinically supports the service. Cite ACEP or specialty guidelines. ER visits for infectious, cardiac, or neurologic diagnoses are almost always upheld. Request peer-to-peer review between your physician and the reviewing physician.

carc.appealNotes: Often a provider coding error. Ask provider to resubmit with corrected modifier. Common with modifier 25, 59, or 51.

UnitedHealthcare Explanation of Benefits

Claim Details

CO-50 N130   99285      ER visit high complexity      $1847.00        $0.00              $0.00         $0.00
PR-1         36415      Routine venipuncture          $45.00          $45.00             $36.00        $9.00

PR-1 Deductible amount patient responsibility

patientFacingSummary: Your insurer denied $2127 that may be wrongful — you have 2 appealable denials with a strong chance of reversal. We've drafted a ready-to-send appeal letter to help you recover this amount.
appealLetter:
**Jane Q. Smith**
[Patient Address]
[City, State ZIP]

**Date:** [Current Date]

**UnitedHealthcare Appeals Department**
[Insurer Address]
[City, State ZIP]

**RE: Level-1 Internal Appeal**
**Claim ID:** 48291
**Member ID:** UHC-2204819334
**Date of Service:** 2026-06-14
**Procedure Code:** 99285

Dear Appeals Review Committee:

I am writing to formally appeal the denial of the above-referenced claim. The Explanation of Benefits indicates denial code CO-50, stating the emergency department service was "not medically necessary."

I respectfully dispute this determination. On June 14, 2026, I presented to Memorial Hospital Emergency Department with symptoms subsequently diagnosed as pneumonia (J18.9). Pneumonia is a potentially life-threatening respiratory infection requiring urgent evaluation to assess severity, rule out sepsis, and determine appropriate treatment. The American College of Emergency Physicians recognizes acute respiratory distress and suspected pneumonia as conditions warranting emergency evaluation. The high medical decision complexity (99285) accurately reflects the clinical workup required, including diagnostic imaging, laboratory studies, and oxygen assessment. Delaying care for outpatient scheduling could have resulted in rapid deterioration or respiratory failure.

**Pursuant to my rights under ERISA §503 and applicable state insurance regulations, I formally request the following information regarding the physician who reviewed and denied this claim:** the reviewer's full name and professional title; medical license number and state(s) of licensure; board certifications and specialty area(s); and confirmation whether they personally reviewed my complete medical record. As this denial constitutes a clinical determination of medical necessity, it must be rendered by a qualified peer physician. If the reviewer lacks relevant expertise in emergency medicine or pulmonology, this constitutes grounds for reversal.

I respectfully request reconsideration of this claim within 30 days. Please contact me with any questions or requests for additional documentation.

Sincerely,

Jane Q. Smith
